items.reader_anchor: The Dietary Guidelines for Americans are developed for the American public but are written for a professional audience, meaning their translation into actionable consumer messages is crucial. (DGA 2020 2025 PMC Summary)

Most meta-analyses of intervention studies associate cholesterol intakes exceeding current average levels with elevated total or low-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentrations. (AHA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Pubmed)
Research has shown that you really cannot isolate dietary cholesterol from that total fat intake. (AHA 2023 Dietary Cholesterol News)
High egg consumption was associated with a higher risk of cardiovascular disease in people with type 2 diabetes. (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)
The association between egg consumption and incident CVD was fully accounted for by the cholesterol content in eggs. (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)

•	49.Herron K.L., Vega-Lopez S., Conde K., Ramjiganesh T., Roy S., Shachter N.S., Fernandez M.L. Pre-menopausal women, classified as hypo- or hyperresponders, do not alter their ldl/hdl ratio following a high dietary cholesterol challenge. J. Am. Coll. Nutr. 2002;21:250–258. doi: 10.1080/07315724.2002.10719218. [DOI] [PubMed] [Google Scholar] (Li 2020 Egg Cholesterol RCT Meta)
Increased egg intake (greater than 1 egg/day) may increase serum LDL cholesterol concentrations. (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)
The associations between egg consumption and incident CVD or all-cause mortality were no longer significant after adjusting for dietary cholesterol consumption. (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)

Total stroke: RR = 0.92 (95% CI 0.84–1.01). In dose-response analysis, 1–4 eggs/week was associated with lower risk and over 10 eggs/week with increased risk. Stroke mortality: RR = 0.84 (95% CI 0.71–1.00). (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)

We used the pooled hazard ratio for cardiovascular disease risk for each one egg per day increase (0.98, 95% confidence interval 0.92 to 1.04) for E value calculations. We estimated that an unmeasured confounder associated with egg consumption and cardiovascular disease by a risk ratio of 1.28-fold each could... (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

For intakes of <3 and ≥3 eggs/week, OR = 1.14 (95% CI 1.07–1.22) and OR = 1.25 (95% CI 1.14–1.38), respectively. For intakes of <3, 3–5, and ≥5 eggs/week, OR = 1.13 (95% CI 1.06–1.21), OR = 1.14 (95% CI 1.01–1.29), and OR = 1.19 (95% CI 1.01–1.39), respectively. In site-specific analyses, egg intake was associated mainly with risk of cancers of the stomach, colon, and colorectum. Increased risk was found only in case–control studies. No associations were found in European studies. (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)
The study cannot determine a dose effect for egg consumption. (Li 2020 Egg Cholesterol RCT Meta)

In the pooled multivariable analysis, consumption of at least one egg per day was not associated with incident cardiovascular disease risk after adjustment for updated lifestyle and dietary factors. (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)
Anyone with a high LDL cholesterol level should consider reducing sources of both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol. (AHA 2023 Dietary Cholesterol News)
The association between egg consumption and cardiovascular disease risk was found to be lower in Asian cohorts compared to US and European cohorts. (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

Compared to no consumption, ≤7 eggs/week associated with lower non-fatal CVD incidence; ≤3 eggs/week with lower non-fatal CHD incidence. No associations with higher intakes or with risk of fatal or non-fatal stroke or with CVD or CHD mortality. Compared to no consumption, ≥7 eggs/week associated with higher HF risk, but only in the one study conducted in the USA. (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)
Pooled relative risk (95% CI) (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

Higher egg intake was associated with a modestly increased risk of breast cancer (RR 1.04, 95% CI 1.01–1.08) (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)

The study population included participants from three large US cohorts (NHS, NHS II, and HPFS) who were free of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and cancer at baseline. (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

CVD = cardiovascular disease; CHD = coronary heart disease; CI = confidence interval; HF = heart failure; OR = odds ratio; RR = relative risk. (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)
The review conclusions depend on the quality of included studies and specific inclusion criteria. (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)

A meta-analysis of 41 prospective cohort studies observed that higher egg intake was associated with a higher risk of total CVD (fatal and non-fatal events combined) (RR = 1.04, 95% CI 1.00–1.08 for each 50 g/day higher egg intake) (13). The association was found mainly in the studies conducted in the USA (RR =... (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)

This analysis included 29 615 participants (mean [SD] age, 51.6 [13.5] years at baseline) of whom 13 299 (44.9%) were men and 9204 (31.1%) were black. During a median follow-up of 17.5 years (interquartile range, 13.0-21.7; maximum, 31.3), there were 5400 incident CVD events and 6132 all-cause deaths. The... (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)
The study findings are observational and cannot establish causality. (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)
The intervention duration of the included studies were all within a year, so results for longer intervention times cannot be concluded. (Li 2020 Egg Cholesterol RCT Meta)

This study included 29 615 participants with 524 376 person-years of follow-up data (Table). Mean (SD) age was 51.6 (13.5) years at baseline, 9204 (31.1%) were black, 13 299 (44.9%) were men. The 6 cohorts differed considerably in terms of sample size, age, sex, race/ethnicity, education level, BMI, and behavioral... (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)
The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and all-cause mortality was stronger in women than in men. (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)

Cardiovascular diseases (CVD), type 2 diabetes (T2D), and cancer are a significant public health burden in the Nordic and Baltic countries. High intake of eggs, mainly due to its high cholesterol content, has been suggested to have adverse health effects. The purpose of this scoping review is to describe the... (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)
The study's findings may not be generalizable to other populations because the cohorts included health professionals. (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and incident CVD was stronger in participants with a BMI lower than 25 than in overweight or obese participants. (JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)

Hyperlipidemia is a phenomenon of increased levels of lipids or triglycerides [20]. It can be classified as hypercholesterolemia, hypertriglyceridemia and elevation of both cholesterol and triglycerides [21]. Higher amounts of LDL-c result in higher risks of cardiovascular diseases. There are more than 100 million... (Li 2020 Egg Cholesterol RCT Meta)
The study focused on healthy subjects to avoid bias from conceptual heterogeneity. (Li 2020 Egg Cholesterol RCT Meta)

(JAMA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Eggs)

Dose/threshold evidence: Systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis (NNR 2023 Eggs Scoping Review)
Guidance focused on dietary patterns is more likely to improve diet quality and promote cardiovascular health than specific dietary cholesterol targets. (AHA 2019 Dietary Cholesterol Pubmed)

Instead of pooling the data, we used fixed effect meta-analysis to estimate the overall association of egg... (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

We also found no apparent differences in results when we used a simple update of diet r... (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)

Dose/threshold evidence: ...was assessed with the I2 statistic and interpreted according to the Cochrane Handbook thresholds (0-40%, might not be important; 30-60%, might represent moderate heterogeneity; 50-90%, might represent substantial heterogeneity; 75-100%, considerable heterogene... (BMJ 2020 Egg Consumption Cvd)
Claim B (observational data) suggests a potential association with CVD risk, while Claim A (intervention meta-analysis) focuses on the physiological response of cholesterol levels to dietary intake.
Claim B specifies that the association between dietary cholesterol and mortality is stronger in women than men, refining the scope of the general finding in Claim A.

Claim A (BMJ) provides a null result for CVD risk with an E-value analysis, while Claim B (LI) shows a significant elevation in the LDL-c/HDL-c ratio. This creates a tension between clinical outcomes and biomarker changes.

Claim A (LI) shows a significant increase in the LDL-c/HDL-c ratio, whereas Claim B (Jama) finds that associations between egg consumption and CVD were no longer significant after adjusting for dietary cholesterol.

Claim A (the specific study cited in Claim B) provides the evidence that pre-menopausal women do not alter their LDL/HDL ratio, which supports the broader scoping review's finding of substantial heterogeneity and complex responses to egg intake.
